perturbation.original_text: In the event of a material breach by either Party of any term of this Agreement, the non-breaching Party may terminate this Agreement by written notice to the breaching Party if the breaching Party fails to cure such material breach within thirty (30) days of receipt of written notice thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of a material breach by either Party of any term of this Agreement, the non-breaching Party may terminate this Agreement by written notice to the breaching Party if the breaching Party fails to cure such material breach within ten (10) days of receipt of written notice thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period for a material breach from 30 days to 10 days may be considered an unreasonably short time frame, potentially conflicting with general contract law principles of providing a reasonable opportunity to cure.
perturbation.law_explanation: This statute relates to warranties on new homes and implies a reasonable time to cure defects. While this contract is not for a new home, reducing the cure period from 30 to 10 days contradicts the concept of a reasonable time to cure found in NY law, creating an unreasonable business practice.
perturbation.justification: The change from a 30-day cure period to a 10-day cure period creates a legal contradiction because it potentially violates the implicit requirement for a 'reasonable' time to cure a breach, as suggested in the Law Explanation, referencing warranties on new homes and reasonable timeframes. Although the scraped snippet relates to warranties on new homes (NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov ... ARTICLE 36-B Warranties On Sales of New Homes), the Law Explanation extends the concept of a 'reasonable time to cure' to general contract law. The shortened cure period could be deemed unreasonable, making the altered contract term potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: FMM shall indicate its acceptance or rejection of the Proposal no later than August 31, 2000. If iVillage does not receive FMM's acceptance or rejection of the Proposal by August 31, 2000, iVillage may interpret FMM's non response as a rejection of the Proposal.
perturbation.changed_text: FMM shall indicate its acceptance or rejection of the Proposal no later than August 7, 2000. If iVillage does not receive FMM's acceptance or rejection of the Proposal by August 7, 2000, iVillage may interpret FMM's non response as a rejection of the Proposal.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the response time to only one week may not be sufficient for companies to properly review and respond to proposals, potentially clashing with good faith and fair dealing implied in contracts.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the UCC primarily applies to the sale of goods, § 2-205 addresses firm offers. Reducing the allowed time for acceptance to just one week could be deemed unreasonable if the offer requires significant review, implying that the offer would not remain open for a period deemed 'reasonable' by commercial standards.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-205. Firm Offers. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-205. Firm Offers. An offer by a merchant to buy or sell goods in a signed writing which by its terms gives assurance that it will be held open is not revocable, for lack of consideration, during the time stated or if no time is stated for a reasonable time, but in no event may such period of irrevocability exceed three months; but any such term of assurance on a form supplied by the offeree must be separately signed by the offeror. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text shortens the response time for a proposal from August 31, 2000, to August 7, 2000. The Law Explanation argues this reduced time might be deemed unreasonable, potentially conflicting with commercial standards for a reasonable response period, especially if the offer requires substantial review. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing UCC § 2-205 on Firm Offers, states that if no time for irrevocability is specified, a 'reasonable time' applies. While the UCC typically applies to goods, the principle of 'reasonable time' for acceptance is relevant. Reducing the acceptance period to one week could be deemed unreasonable in a commercial context, thus creating a contradiction if this timeframe is insufficient for proper review, and this violates the assurance of a reasonable time period per UCC § 2-205, especially if the proposal requires significant review.

perturbation.original_text: Once presented with an opportunity, Hertz shall have five (5) business days in which to accept or reject such terms. If iVillage does not receive Hertz's acceptance or rejection of such within the allotted time, iVillage shall deem Hertz's silence as rejection.
perturbation.changed_text: Once presented with an opportunity, Hertz shall have two (2) business days in which to accept or reject such terms. If iVillage does not receive Hertz's acceptance or rejection of such within the allotted time, iVillage shall deem Hertz's silence as rejection.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the acceptance period to two days imposes an extremely tight deadline that may not provide Hertz with reasonable time to evaluate the sponsorship opportunity. This may constitute a violation of the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Uniform Commercial Code, Section 1-304
perturbation.law_explanation: The change from five to two days for acceptance by Hertz imposes an unreasonably short time, potentially violating the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing required by NY UCC 1-304. This provision says that 'Every contract or duty within [the Uniform Commercial Code] imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement'.

Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The change from five to two business days for acceptance by Hertz creates an inconsistency regarding the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. According to the provided snippets from NY UCC 1-304, 'Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' Reducing the time so drastically could be interpreted as not providing Hertz with a reasonable opportunity, thus violating this obligation. The altered text directly contradicts the implied duty of good faith within the UCC, as the shortened time frame could be deemed unreasonable and therefore a violation.
